entries.title: Proverbs — Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 113 Psa 113:1-9. God's majesty contrasted with His condescension and gracious dealings towards the humble furnish matter and a call for praise. The Jews, it is said, used this and Psalms 114-118 on their great festivals, and called them the Greater Hallel, or Hymn. 1-3. Earnestness and zeal are denoted by the emphatic repetitions. servants of the Lord-- or, all the people of God. name of the Lord-- perfections (Psa 5:11; 111:9).

entries.title: Proverbs 1:2–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-6. God's exaltation enhances His condescension;

entries.title: Proverbs 1:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. which condescension is illustrated as often in raising the worthy poor and needy to honor (compare 1Sa 2:8; Psa 44:25).

entries.title: Proverbs 1:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. On this special case, compare 1Sa 2:21. Barrenness was regarded as a disgrace, and is a type of a deserted Church (Is 54:1). the barren woman ... house-- literally, "the barren of the house," so that the supplied words may be omitted.

entries.title: Proverbs 1:9–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 114 Psa 114:1-8. The writer briefly and beautifully celebrates God's former care of His people, to whose benefit nature was miraculously made to contribute. 1-4. of strange language-- (compare Psa 81:5).

entries.title: Proverbs 1:13–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-8. The questions place the implied answers in a more striking form.

entries.title: Proverbs 1:18–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 115 Psa 115:1-18. The Psalmist prays that God would vindicate His glory, which is contrasted with the vanity of idols, while the folly of their worshippers is contrasted with the trust of God's people, who are encouraged to its exercise and to unite in the praise which it occasions. 1-3. The vindication of God's mercy and faithfulness (Psa 25:10; 36:6) is the "glory" of His "name," which is desired to be illustrated in the deliverance of His people, as the implied mode of its manifestation. In view of the taunts of the heathen, faith in His dominion as enthroned in the heaven (Psa 2:4; 11:4) is avowed.

entries.title: Proverbs 1:21–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 1:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. every one that trusteth-- they who trust, whether makers or not.

entries.title: Proverbs 1:26–30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9-13. The repetitions imply earnestness.

entries.title: Proverbs 1:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. Opposed to the decrease pending and during the captivity.

entries.title: Proverbs 1:32–2:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-17. They were not only God's peculiar people, but as living inhabitants of earth, assigned the work of His praise as monuments of divine power, wisdom, and goodness.

entries.title: Proverbs 2:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. Hence let us fulfil the purpose of our creation, and evermore show forth His praise.

entries.title: Proverbs 2:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 116 Psa 116:1-19. The writer celebrates the deliverance from extreme perils by which he was favored, and pledges grateful and pious public acknowledgments. 1-2. A truly grateful love will be evinced by acts of worship, which calling on God expresses (Psa 116:13; Psa 55:16; 86:7; compare Psa 17:6; 31:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 2:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. For similar figures for distress see Psa 18:4, 5. gat hold upon me-- Another sense ("found") of the same word follows, as we speak of disease finding us, and of our finding or catching disease.

entries.title: Proverbs 2:7–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-8. The relief which he asked is the result not of his merit, but of God's known pity and tenderness, which is acknowledged in assuring himself (his "soul," Psa 11:1; 16:10) of rest and peace. All calamities [Psa 116:8] are represented by death, tears, and falling of the feet (Psa 56:13).

entries.title: Proverbs 2:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. walk before the Lord-- act, or live under His favor and guidance (Ge 17:1; Psa 61:7). land of the living-- (Psa 27:13).

entries.title: Proverbs 2:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. Confidence in God opposed to distrust of men, as not reliable (Psa 68:8, 9). He speaks from an experience of the result of his faith.

entries.title: Proverbs 2:14–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-14. These are modes of expressing acts of worship (compare Psa 116:4; Psa 50:14; Jon 2:9).

entries.title: Proverbs 2:17–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. By the plea of being a homeborn servant, he intimates his claim on God's covenant love to His people.

entries.title: Proverbs 2:19–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-19. An ampler declaration of his purpose, designating the place, the Lord's house, or earthly residence in Jerusalem.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 117 Psa 117:1, 2. This may be regarded as a doxology, suitable to be appended to any Psalm of similar character, and prophetical of the prevalence of God's grace in the world, in which aspect Paul quotes it (Ro 15:11; compare Psa 47:2; 66:8). 2. is great toward us-- literally, "prevailed over" or "protected us."

entries.title: Proverbs 3:3–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 118 Psa 118:1-29. After invoking others to unite in praise, the writer celebrates God's protecting and delivering care towards him, and then represents himself and the people of God as entering the sanctuary and uniting in solemn praise, with prayer for a continued blessing. Whether composed by David on his accession to power, or by some later writer in memory of the restoration from Babylon, its tone is joyful and trusting, and, in describing the fortune and destiny of the Jewish Church and its visible head, it is typically prophetical of the Christian Church and her greater and invisible Head. 1-4. The trine repetitions are emphatic (compare Psa 118:10-12, 15, 16; 115:12, 13). Let ... say-- Oh! that Israel may say. now-- as in Psa 115:2; so in Psa 118:3, 4. After "now say" supply "give thanks." that his mercy-- or, "for His mercy."

entries.title: Proverbs 3:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. distress-- literally, "straits," to which "large place" corresponds, as in Psa 4:1; 31:8.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. Men are helpless to hurt him, if God be with him (Psa 56:9), and, if enemies, they will be vanquished (Psa 54:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 3:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. Even the most powerful men are less to be trusted than God.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:12–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-12. Though as numerous and irritating as bees [Psa 118:12], by God's help his enemies would be destroyed.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:15–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-16. The enemy is triumphantly addressed as if present.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-18. He would live, because confident his life would be for God's glory.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:21–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-21. Whether an actual or figurative entrance into God's house be meant, the purpose of solemn praise is intimated, in which only the righteous would or could engage.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:24–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-23. These words are applied by Christ (Mt 21:42) to Himself, as the foundation of the Church (compare Ac 4:11; Ep 2:20; 1Pe 2:4, 7). It may here denote God's wondrous exaltation to power and influence of him whom the rulers of the nation despised. Whether (see on Psa 118:1) David or Zerubbabel (compare Hag 2:2; Zec 4:7-10) be primarily meant, there is here typically represented God's more wonderful doings in exalting Christ, crucified as an impostor, to be the Prince and Saviour and Head of His Church.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. This is the day-- or period distinguished by God's favor of all others.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. Save now-- Hebrew, "Hosanna" (compare Psa 115:2, &c., as to now) a form of prayer (Psa 20:9), since, in our use, of praise.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. he that cometh ... Lord-- As above intimated, this may be applied to the visible head of the Jewish Church entering the sanctuary, as leading the procession; typically it belongs to Him of whom the phrase became an epithet (Mal 3:1; Mt 21:9).

entries.title: Proverbs 3:29–31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27-29. showed us light-- or favor (Psa 27:1; 97:11). With the sacrificial victim brought bound to the altar is united the more spiritual offering of praise (Psa 50:14, 23), expressed in the terms with which the Psalm opened.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 119 Psa 119:1-176. This celebrated Psalm has several peculiarities. It is divided into twenty-two parts or stanzas, denoted by the twenty-two letters of the Hebrew alphabet. Each stanza contains eight verses, and the first letter of each verse is that which gives name to the stanza. Its contents are mainly praises of God's Word, exhortations to its perusal, and reverence for it, prayers for its proper influence, and complaints of the wicked for despising it. There are but two verses (Psa 119:122, 132) which do not contain some term or description of God's Word. These terms are of various derivations, but here used, for the most part, synonymously, though the use of a variety of terms seems designed, in order to express better the several aspects in which our relations to the revealed word of God are presented. The Psalm does not appear to have any relation to any special occasion or interest of the Jewish Church or nation, but was evidently "intended as a manual of pious thoughts, especially for instructing the young, and its peculiar artificial structure was probably adopted to aid the memory in retaining the language." ALEPH. (Psa 119:1-8). 1. undefiled-- literally, "complete," perfect, or sincere (compare Psa 37:37). in-- or, "of" the way-- course of life. walk-- act in the law-- according to it (compare Lu 1:6). law-- from a word meaning "to teach," is a term of rather general purport, denoting the instruction of God's Word.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. testimonies-- The word of God is so called, because in it He testifies for truth and against sin. seek him-- that is, a knowledge of Him, with desire for conformity to His will.

entries.title: Proverbs 3:35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. his ways-- the course He reveals as right.

entries.title: Proverbs 4 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-6. precepts-- are those directions which relate to special conduct, from a word meaning "to inspect." statutes-- or ordinances, positive laws of permanent nature. Both words originally denote rather positive than moral laws, such as derive force from the divine appointment, whether their nature or the reasons for them are apprehended by us or not. commandments-- or institutions. The term is comprehensive, but rather denotes fundamental directions for conduct, both enjoining and forbidding. have respect unto-- or regard carefully as to their whole purport.

entries.title: Proverbs 4:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. judgments-- rules of conduct formed by God's judicial decisions; hence the wide sense of the word in the Psalms, so that it includes decisions of approval as well as condemnation.

entries.title: Proverbs 4:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Recognizes the need of divine grace. BETH. (Psa 119:9-16).

entries.title: Proverbs 4:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. The whole verse may be read as a question; for, by taking heed-- is better, "for" taking heed, that is, so as to do it. The answer is implied, and inferable from Psa 119:5, 10, 18, &c., that is, by God's grace.

entries.title: Proverbs 4:6–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-16. We must carefully treasure up the word of God, declare it to others, meditate on it, and heartily delight in it; and then by His grace we shall act according to it. GIMEL. (Psa 119:17-24).

entries.title: Proverbs 4:13–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17-20. Life is desirable in order to serve God; that we may do so aright, we should seek to have our eyes opened to behold His truth, and earnestly desire fully to understand it.

entries.title: Proverbs 4:17–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21-24. God will rebuke those who despise His word and deliver His servants from their reproach, giving them boldness in and by His truth, even before the greatest men. DALETH. (Psa 119:25-32).

entries.title: Proverbs 4:21–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25-27. Submitting ourselves in depression to God, He will revive us by His promises, and lead us to declare His mercy to others.

entries.title: Proverbs 4:24–5:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28-32. In order to adhere to His word, we must seek deliverance from temptations to sin as well as from despondency. enlarge-- or, "expand" my heart-- with gracious affections. HE. (Psa 119:33-40).

entries.title: Proverbs 5:1–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 33-38. To encourage us in prayer for divine aid in adhering to His truth, we are permitted to believe that by His help we shall succeed. the way of thy statutes-- that is, the way or manner of life prescribed by them. The help we hope to obtain by prayer is to be the basis on which our resolutions should rest.

entries.title: Proverbs 5:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 39-40. Our hope of freedom from the reproach of inconsistency is in God's power, quickening us to live according to His Word, which He leads us to love. for thy judgments are good-- The time must therefore be at hand when Thy justice will turn the "reproach" from Thy Church upon the world (Is 25:8; 66:5; Zep 2:8-10). VAU. (Psa 119:41-48).

entries.title: Proverbs 5:9–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 41-44. The sentiment more fully carried out. God's mercies and salvation, as revealed in His Word, provide hope of forgiveness for the past and security in a righteous course for the future.

entries.title: Proverbs 5:13–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 45-48. To freedom from reproach, when imbued with God's truth, there is added "great boldness in the faith" [1Ti 3:13], accompanied with increasing delight in the holy law itself, which becomes an element of happiness.

entries.title: Proverbs 5:17–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 49-51. Resting on the promises consoles under affliction and the tauntings of the insolent. upon which-- rather, "Remember Thy word unto Thy servant, because," &c. So the Hebrew requires [Hengstenberg].

entries.title: Proverbs 5:20–6:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 52-56. The pious take comfort, when harassed and distressed by wickedness of men who forsake God's law, in remembering that the great principles of God's truth will still abide; and also God's judgments of old-- that is, His past interpositions in behalf of His people are a pledge that He will again interpose to deliver them; and they become the theme of constant and delightful meditation. The more we keep the more we love the law of God.

entries.title: Proverbs 6:1–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 57-60. Sincere desires for God's favor, penitence, and activity in a new obedience, truly evince the sincerity of those who profess to find God a portion (Nu 18:20; Psa 16:5; La 3:24).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 61-62. This the more, if opposition of enemies, or love of ease is overcome in thus honoring God's law. have robbed me-- better, surrounded me, either as forcible constraints like fetters, or as the cords of their nets. Hengstenberg translates, "snares."

entries.title: Proverbs 6:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 63. The communion of the saints. Delight in their company is an evidence of belonging to them (Psa 16:3; Am 3:3; Mal 3:16).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 64. While opposed by the wicked, and opposing them, the pious delight in those who fear God, but, after all, rely for favor and guidance not on merit, but mercy. TETH. (Psa 119:65-72).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:9–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 65-67. The reliance on promises (Psa 119:49) is strengthened by experience of past dealings according with promises, and a prayer for guidance, encouraged by sanctified affliction.

entries.title: Proverbs 6:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 68. Compare as to the Lord Jesus (Ac 10:38).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 69-70. The crafty malice of the wicked, in slandering him, so far from turning him away, but binds him closer to God's Word, which they are too stupid in sin to appreciate. Hengstenberg refers the "lie" to such slanders against the Jews during the captivity, as that in Ezr 4:1-6, of sedition.

entries.title: Proverbs 6:15–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 71-72. So also affliction of any kind acts as a wholesome discipline in leading the pious more highly to value the truth and promises of God. JOD. (Psa 119:73-80).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 73. As God made, so He can best control, us. So as to Israel, he owed to God his whole internal and external existence (De 32:6).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 74. So when He has led us to rely on His truth, He will "make us to the praise of His grace" by others. "Those who fear Thee will be glad at my prosperity, as they consider my cause their cause" (Psa 34:2; 142:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:19–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 75-78. in faithfulness-- that is, without in the least violating Thy faithfulness; because my sins deserved and needed fatherly chastisement. Enduring chastisement with a filial temper (He 12:6-11), God's promises of mercy (Ro 8:28) will be fulfilled, and He will give comfort in sorrow (La 3:22; 2Co 1:3, 4).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 79-80. Those who may have thought his afflictions an evidence of God's rejection will then be led to return to Him; as the friends of Job did on his restoration, having been previously led through his afflictions to doubt the reality of his religion.

entries.title: Proverbs 6:25–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 81-83. In sorrow the pious heart yearns for the comforts of God's promises (Psa 73:26; 84:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 6:28–31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 84-87. The shortness of my life requires that the relief afforded to me from mine enemies should be speedy.

entries.title: Proverbs 6:33–35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 89-91. In all changes God's Word remains firm (1Pe 1:25). Like the heavens, it continually attests God's unfailing power and unchanging care (Psa 89:2). is settled in-- that is, stands as firmly as the heaven in which it dwells, and whence it emanated.

entries.title: Proverbs 7 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 92-94. Hence the pious are encouraged and inclined to seek a knowledge of it, and persevere amidst the efforts of those planning and waiting to destroy them. my delights-- plural, not merely delight, but equal to all other delights.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 97. This characteristic love for God's law (compare Psa 1:2) ensures increase.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 98-100. of knowledge, both of the matter of all useful, moral truth, and an experience of its application. wiser than mine enemies-- with all their carnal cunning (De 4:6, 8). they are ever with me-- The Hebrew is, rather singular, "it is ever with me"; the commandments forming ONE complete whole, Thy law.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:9–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 101-104. Avoidance of sinful courses is both the effect and means of increasing in divine knowledge (compare Psa 19:10). NUN. (Psa 119:105-112).

entries.title: Proverbs 7:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 105. Not only does the Word of God inform us of His will, but, as a light on a path in darkness, it shows us how to follow the right and avoid the wrong way. The lamp of the Word is not the sun. He would blind our eyes in our present fallen state; but we may bless God for the light shining as in a dark place, to guide us until the Sun of Righteousness shall come, and we shall be made capable of seeing Him (2Pe 1:19; Re 22:4). The lamp is fed with the oil of the Spirit. The allusion is to the lamps and torches carried at night before an Eastern caravan.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:14–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 106-108. Such was the national covenant at Sinai and in the fields of Moab.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:17–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 109-110. In the midst of deadly perils (the phrase is drawn from the fact that what we carry in our hands may easily slip from them, Jud 12:3; 1Sa 28:21; Job 13:14; compare 1Sa 19:5), and exposed to crafty enemies, his safety and guidance is in the truth and promises of God.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 111-112. These he joyfully takes as his perpetual heritage, to perform the duties and receive the comforts they teach, evermore. SAMECH. (Psa 119:113-120).

entries.title: Proverbs 7:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 113. vain thoughts-- better, "unstable persons," literally, "divided men," those of a divided, doubting mind (Jas 1:8); "a double-minded man" [Hengstenberg], skeptics, or, skeptical notions as opposed to the certainty of God's word.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 114. hiding-place-- (Compare Psa 27:5). shield-- (Psa 3:3; 7:10). hope in thy word-- confidently rest on its teachings and promises.

entries.title: Proverbs 7:23–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 115-117. Hence he fears not wicked men, nor dreads disappointment, sustained by God in making His law the rule of life. Depart from me-- Ye can do nothing with me; for, &c. (Psa 6:8).

entries.title: Proverbs 7:26–8:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 118-120. But the disobedient and rebellious will be visited by God's wrath, which impresses the pious with wholesome fear and awe. their deceit is falsehood-- that is, all their cunning deceit, wherewith they seek to entrap the godly, is in vain.

entries.title: Proverbs 8:1–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 121-126. On the grounds of his integrity, desire for God's word, and covenant relation to Him, the servant of God may plead for His protecting care against the wicked, gracious guidance to the knowledge of truth, and His effective vindication of the righteous and their cause, which is also His own.

entries.title: Proverbs 8:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 127-128. Therefore-- that is, In view of these benefits, or, Because of the glory of Thy law, so much praised in the previous parts of the Psalm. I love ... [and] Therefore (repeated)-- All its precepts, on all subjects, are estimable for their purity, and lead one imbued with their spirit to hate all evil (Psa 19:10). The Word of God admits of no eclecticism; its least title is perfect (Psa 12:6; Mt 5:17-19). PE. (Psa 119:129-136).

entries.title: Proverbs 8:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 129. wonderful-- literally, "wonders," that is, of moral excellence.

entries.title: Proverbs 8:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 130. The entrance-- literally, "opening"; God's words, as an open door, let in light, or knowledge. Rather, as Hengstenberg explains it, "The opening up," or, "explanation of thy word." To the natural man the doors of God's Word are shut. Lu 24:27, 31; Ac 17:3; Ep 1:18, confirm this view, "opening (that is, explaining) and alleging," &c. unto the simple-- those needing or desiring it (compare Psa 19:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 8:11–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 131-135. An ardent desire (compare Psa 56:1, 2) for spiritual enlightening, establishment in a right course, deliverance from the wicked, and evidence of God's favor is expressed I opened my mouth, and panted-- as a traveller in a hot desert pants for the cooling breeze (Psa 63:1; 84:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 8:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 136. Zealous himself to keep God's law, he is deeply afflicted when others violate it (compare Psa 119:53). Literally, "Mine eyes come down (dissolved) like water brooks" (La 3:48; Jr 9:1). because, &c.-- (Compare Eze 9:4; Jr 13:17). TZADDI. (Psa 119:137-144).

entries.title: Proverbs 8:17–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 137-139. God's justice and faithfulness in His government aggravate the neglect of the wicked, and more excite the lively zeal of His people.

entries.title: Proverbs 8:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 140. very pure-- literally, "refined," shown pure by trial.

entries.title: Proverbs 8:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 141. The pious, however despised of men, are distinguished in God's sight by a regard for His law.

entries.title: Proverbs 8:22–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 142-144. The principles of God's government are permanent and reliable, and in the deepest distress His people find them a theme of delightful meditation and a source of reviving power (Psa 119:17, 116). law is the truth-- It therefore cannot deceive as to its promises. everlasting-- (Psa 111:3), though to outward appearance seeming dead. KOPH. (Psa 119:145-152).

entries.title: Proverbs 8:25–29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 145-149. An intelligent devotion is led by divine promises and is directed to an increase of gracious affections, arising from a contemplation of revealed truth.

entries.title: Proverbs 8:30–32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 150-152. Though the wicked are near to injure, because far from God's law, He is near to help, and faithful to His word, which abides for ever. RESH. (Psa 119:153-160).

entries.title: Proverbs 8:33–35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 153-155. Though the remembering of God's law is not meritorious, yet it evinces a filial temper and provides the pious with promises to plead, while the wicked in neglecting His law, reject God and despise His promises (compare Psa 9:13; 43:1; 69:18).

entries.title: Proverbs 8:36
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 9 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 9:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 158. (Compare Psa 119:136). transgressors-- or, literally, "traitors," who are faithless to a righteous sovereign and side with His enemies (compare Psa 25:3, 8).

entries.title: Proverbs 9:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 159. (Compare Psa 119:121-126, 153-155). quicken me, O Lord, according to thy lovingkindness-- (Psa 119:88). This prayer occurs here for the ninth time, showing a deep sense of frailty.

entries.title: Proverbs 9:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 160. God has been ever faithful, and the principles of His government will ever continue worthy of confidence. from the beginning-- that is, "every word from Genesis (called so by the Jews from its first words, 'In the beginning') to the end of the Scriptures is true." Hengstenberg translates more literally, "The sum of thy words is truth." The sense is substantially the same. The whole body of revelation is truth. "Thy Word is nothing but truth" [Luther]. SCHIN. (Psa 119:161-168).

entries.title: Proverbs 9:4–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 161-165. (Compare Psa 119:46, 86). awe-- reverential, not slavish fear, which could not coexist with love (Psa 119:163; 1Jo 4:8). Instead of fearing his persecutors, he fears God's Word alone (Lu 12:4, 5). The Jews inscribe in the first page of the great Bible (Ge 28:17), "How dreadful is this place! This is none other but the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven!"

entries.title: Proverbs 9:9–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 166-168. As they keep God's law from motives of love for it, and are free from slavish fear, the are ready to subject their lives to His inspection.

entries.title: Proverbs 9:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 169-170. The prayer for understanding of the truth precedes that for deliverance. The fulfilment of the first is the basis of the fulfilment of the second (Psa 90:11-17). On the terms "cry" and "supplication" (compare Psa 6:9; 17:1).

entries.title: Proverbs 9:14–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 171-172. shall utter-- or, "pour out praise" (compare Psa 19:2); shall cause Thy praises to stream forth as from a bubbling, overflowing fountain.

entries.title: Proverbs 9:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 173-174. (Compare Psa 119:77, 81, 92). I have chosen-- in preference to all other objects of delight.

entries.title: Proverbs 9:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 175. Save me that I may praise Thee. thy judgments-- as in Psa 119:149, 156.

entries.title: Proverbs 10 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 176. Though a wanderer from God, the truly pious ever desires to be drawn back to Him; and, though for a time negligent of duty, he never forgets the commandments by which it is taught. lost-- therefore utterly helpless as to recovering itself (Jr 50:6; Lu 15:4). Not only the sinner before conversion, but the believer after conversion, is unable to recover himself; but the latter, after temporary wandering, knows to whom to look for restoration. Psa 119:175, 176 seem to sum up the petitions, confessions, and professions of the Psalm. The writer desires God's favor, that he may praise Him for His truth, confesses that he has erred, but, in the midst of all his wanderings and adversities, professes an abiding attachment to the revealed Word of God, the theme of such repeated eulogies, and the recognized source of such great and unnumbered blessings. Thus the Psalm, though more than usually didactic, is made the medium of both parts of devotion--prayer and praise.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:3–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 120 Psa 120:1-7. This is the first of fifteen Psalms (Psalms 120-134) entitled "A Song of Degrees" (Psa 121:1--literally, "A song for the degrees"), or ascents. It seems most probable they were designed for the use of the people when going up (compare 1Ki 12:27, 28) to Jerusalem on the festival occasions (De 16:16), three times a year. David appears as the author of four, Solomon of one (Psa 127:1), and the other ten are anonymous, probably composed after the captivity. In this Psalm the writer acknowledges God's mercy, prays for relief from a malicious foe, whose punishment he anticipates, and then repeats his complaint. 2-3. Slander and deceit charged on his foes implies his innocence. tongue-- as in Psa 52:2, 4.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Sharp arrows of the mighty-- destructive inflictions. coals of juniper-- which retain heat long. This verse may be read as a description of the wicked, but better as their punishment, in reply to the question of Psa 120:3.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. A residence in these remote lands pictures his miserable condition.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. While those who surrounded him were maliciously hostile, he was disposed to peace. This Psalm may well begin such a series as this, as a contrast to the promised joys of God's worship.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 121 Psa 121:1-8. God's guardian care of His people celebrated. 1. I will lift up mine eyes-- expresses desire (compare Psa 25:1), mingled with expectation. The last clause, read as a question, is answered,

entries.title: Proverbs 10:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. by avowing God to be the helper, of whose ability His creative power is a pledge (Psa 115:15), to which,

entries.title: Proverbs 10:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. His sleepless vigilance is added. to be moved-- (Compare Psa 38:16; 66:9).

entries.title: Proverbs 10:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. upon thy right hand-- a protector's place (Psa 109:31; 110:5).

entries.title: Proverbs 10:15–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-8. God keeps His people at all times and in all perils. nor the moon by night-- poetically represents the dangers of the night, over which the moon presides (Ge 1:16).

entries.title: Proverbs 10:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 122 Psa 122:1-9. This Psalm might well express the sacred joy of the pilgrims on entering the holy city, where praise, as the religious as well as civil metropolis, is celebrated, and for whose prosperity, as representing the Church, prayer is offered. 1-2. Our feet shall stand-- literally, "are standing."

entries.title: Proverbs 10:21–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-5. compact together-- all parts united, as in David's time.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:24–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. Let peace-- including prosperity, everywhere prevail.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. In the welfare of the city, as its civil, and especially the religious relations, was involved that of Israel. now-- as in Psa 115:2.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 123 Psa 123:1-4. An earnest and expecting prayer for divine aid in distress. 1. (Compare Psa 121:1). thou that dwellest-- literally, "sittest as enthroned" (compare Psa 2:4; 113:4, 5).

entries.title: Proverbs 10:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Deference, submission, and trust, are all expressed by the figure. In the East, servants in attending on their masters are almost wholly directed by signs, which require the closest observance of the hands of the latter. The servants of God should look (1) to His directing hand, to appoint them their work; (2) to His supplying hand (Psa 104:28), to give them their portion in due season; (3) to His protecting hand, to right them when wronged; (4) to His correcting hand (Is 9:13; 1Pe 5:6; compare Ge 16:6); (5) to His rewarding hand.

entries.title: Proverbs 10:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. contempt-- was that of the heathen, and, perhaps, Samaritans (Ne 1:3; 2:19).

entries.title: Proverbs 10:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. of those that are at ease-- self-complacently, disregarding God's law, and despising His people.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 124 Psa 124:1-8. The writer, for the Church, praises God for past, and expresses trust for future, deliverance from foes. 1-2. on our side-- for us (Psa 56:9). now-- or, "oh! let Israel"

entries.title: Proverbs 11:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Then-- that is, the time of our danger. quick-- literally, "living" (Nu 16:32, 33), description of ferocity.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 11:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. The figure is changed to that of a rapacious wild beast (Psa 3:7), and then of a fowler (Psa 91:3), and complete escape is denoted by breaking the net.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. (Compare Psa 121:2). name-- in the usual sense (Psa 5:11; 20:1). He thus places over against the great danger the omnipotent God, and drowns, as it were in an anthem, the wickedness of the whole world and of hell, just as a great fire consumes a little drop of water [Luther].

entries.title: Proverbs 11:10–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 125 Psa 125:1-5. God honors the confidence of His people, by protection and deliverance, and leaves hypocrites to the doom of the wicked. 1-2. Mount Zion-- as an emblem of permanence, and locality of Jerusalem as one of security, represent the firm and protected condition of God's people (compare Psa 46:5), supported not only by Providence, but by covenant promise. Even the mountains shall depart, and the hills be removed, but God's kindness shall not depart, nor His covenant of peace be removed (Is 54:10). They that trust-- are "His people," (Psa 125:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 11:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Though God may leave them for a time under the "rod," or power (Psa 2:9), and oppression of the wicked for a time, as a chastisement, He will not suffer them to be tempted so as to fall into sin (1Co 10:13). The wicked shall only prove a correcting rod to them, not a destroying sword; even this rod shall not remain ("rest") on them, lest they be tempted to despair and apostasy (Psa 73:13, 14). God may even try His people to the uttermost: when nothing is before our eyes but pure despair, then He delivers us and gives life in death, and makes us blessed in the curse (2Co 1:8, 9) [Luther]. the lot-- the possession, literally, "Canaan," spiritually, the heavenly inheritance of holiness and bliss which is appointed to the righteous. Sin's dominion shall not permanently come between the believer and his inheritance.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 11:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Those who turn aside (under temptation) permanently show that they are hypocrites, and their lot or portion shall be with the wicked (Psa 28:3). crooked ways-- (Compare De 9:16; Mal 2:8, 9). their-- is emphatic; the "crooked ways" proceed from their own hearts. The true Israel is here distinguished from the false. Scripture everywhere opposes the Jewish delusion that mere outward descent would save (Ro 2:28, 29; 9:6, 7; Ga 6:16). The byways of sin from the way of life.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:16–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 126 Psa 126:1-6. To praise for God's favor to His people is added a prayer for its continued manifestation. 1-3. When the Lord, &c.-- The joy of those returned from Babylon was ecstatic, and elicited the admiration even of the heathen, as illustrating God's great power and goodness. turned again the captivity-- that is, restored from it (Job 39:12; Psa 14:7; Pr 12:14). Hengstenberg translates: "When the Lord turned Himself to the turning of Zion" (see Margin), God returns to His people when they return to Him (De 30:2, 3).

entries.title: Proverbs 11:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. All did not return at once; hence the prayer for repeated favors. as the streams in the south-- or, the torrents in the desert south of Judea, dependent on rain (Jos 15:9), reappearing after dry seasons (compare Job 6:15; Psa 68:9). The point of comparison is joy at the reappearing of what has been so painfully missed.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:20–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. As in husbandry the sower may cast his seed in a dry and parched soil with desponding fears, so those shall reap abundant fruit who toil in tears with the prayer of faith. (Compare the history, Ezr 6:16, 22).

entries.title: Proverbs 11:23–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 127 Psa 127:1-5. The theme of this Psalm, that human enterprises only succeed by the divine blessing, was probably associated with the building of the temple by Solomon, its author. It may have been adopted in this view, as suited to this series especially, as appropriately expressing the sentiments of God's worshippers in relation to the erection of the second temple. 1-2. suggest the view of the theme given.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:25–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-5. Posterity is often represented as a blessing from God (Ge 30:2, 18; 1Sa 1:19, 20). Children are represented as the defenders (arrows) of their parents in war, and in litigation.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 128 Psa 128:1-6. The temporal blessings of true piety. The eighth chapter of Zecariah is a virtual commentary on this Psalm. Compare Psa 128:3 with Zec 8:5; and Psa 128:2 with Le 26:16; De 28:33; Zec 8:10; and Psa 128:6 with Zec 8:4. 1. (Compare Psa 1:1).

entries.title: Proverbs 11:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. For thou shalt eat-- that is, It is a blessing to live on the fruits of one's own industry.

entries.title: Proverbs 11:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. by the sides-- or, "within" (Psa 48:2). olive plants-- are peculiarly luxuriant (Psa 52:8).

entries.title: Proverbs 12:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. In temporal blessings the pious do not forget the richer blessings of God's grace, which they shall ever enjoy.

entries.title: Proverbs 12:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Long life crowns all other temporal favors. As Psa 125:5, this Psalm closes with a prayer for peace, with prosperity for God's people.

entries.title: Proverbs 12:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 129 Psa 129:1-8. The people of God, often delivered from enemies, are confident of His favor, by their overthrow in the future. 1-2. may Israel now say-- or, "oh! let Israel say" (Psa 124:1). Israel's youth was the sojourn in Egypt (Jr 2:2; Ho 2:15).

entries.title: Proverbs 12:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. The ploughing is a figure of scourging, which most severe physical infliction aptly represents all kinds.

entries.title: Proverbs 12:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. The ill-rooted roof grass, which withers before it grows up and procures for those gathering it no harvest blessing (Ru 2:4), sets forth the utter uselessness and the rejection of the wicked.

entries.title: Proverbs 12:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 130 Psa 130:1-8. The penitent sinner's hope is in God's mercy only. 1-2. depths-- for great distress (Psa 40:2; 69:3).

entries.title: Proverbs 12:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. shouldest mark-- or, "take strict account" (Job 10:14; 14:16), implying a confession of the existence of sin. who shall stand-- (Psa 1:6). Standing is opposed to the guilty sinking down in fear and self-condemnation (Mal 3:2; Re 6:15, 16). The question implies a negative, which is thus more strongly stated.

entries.title: Proverbs 12:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Pardon produces filial fear and love. Judgment without the hope of pardon creates fear and dislike. The sense of forgiveness, so far from producing licentiousness, produces holiness (Jr 33:9; Eze 16:62, 63; 1Pe 2:16). "There is forgiveness with thee, not that thou mayest be presumed upon, but feared."

entries.title: Proverbs 12:17–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. wait for the Lord-- in expectation (Psa 27:14). watch for, &c.-- in earnestness and anxiety.

entries.title: Proverbs 12:19–20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. Let Israel, &c.-- that is, All are invited to seek and share divine forgiveness. from all his iniquities-- or, "punishments of them" (Psa 40:12, &c.).

entries.title: Proverbs 12:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 131 Psa 131:1-3. This Psalm, while expressive of David's pious feelings on assuming the royal office, teaches the humble, submissive temper of a true child of God. 1. eyes lofty-- a sign of pride (Psa 18:27). exercise myself-- literally, "walk in," or "meddle with."

entries.title: Proverbs 12:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Surely, &c.-- The form is that of an oath or strongest assertion. Submission is denoted by the figure of a weaned child. As the child weaned by his mother from the breast, so I still the motions of pride in me (Mt 18:3, 4; Is 11:8; 28:9). Hebrew children were often not weaned till three years old. soul-- may be taken for desire, which gives a more definite sense, though one included in the idea conveyed by the usual meaning, myself.

entries.title: Proverbs 12:26–13:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 132 Psa 132:1-18. The writer, perhaps Solomon (compare Psa 132:8, 9), after relating David's pious zeal for God's service, pleads for the fulfilment of the promise (2Sa 7:16), which, providing for a perpetuation of David's kingdom, involved that of God's right worship and the establishment of the greater and spiritual kingdom of David's greater Son. Of Him and His kingdom both the temple and its worship, and the kings and kingdom of Judah, were types. The congruity of such a topic with the tenor of this series of Psalms is obvious. 1-5. This vow is not elsewhere recorded. It expresses, in strong language, David's intense desire to see the establishment of God's worship as well as of His kingdom. remember David-- literally, "remember for David," that is, all his troubles and anxieties on the matter.

entries.title: Proverbs 13:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. These may be the "words of David" and his pious friends, who, at Ephratah-- or Beth-lehem (Ge 48:7), where he once lived, may have heard of the ark, which he found for the first time in the fields of the wood-- or, Jair, or Kirjath-jearim ("City of woods") (1Sa 7:1; 2Sa 6:3, 4), whence it was brought to Zion.

entries.title: Proverbs 13:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The purpose of engaging in God's worship is avowed.

entries.title: Proverbs 13:4–5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. The solemn entry of the ark, symbolical of God's presence and power, with the attending priests, into the sanctuary, is proclaimed in the words used by Solomon (2Ch 6:41).

entries.title: Proverbs 13:6–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-12. For thy servant David's sake-- that is, On account of the promise made to him. turn ... anointed-- Repulse not him who, as David's descendant, pleads the promise to perpetuate his royal line. After reciting the promise, substantially from 2Sa 7:12-16 (compare Ac 2:30, &c.), an additional plea,

entries.title: Proverbs 13:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. is made on the ground of God's choice of Zion (here used for Jerusalem) as His dwelling, inasmuch as the prosperity of the kingdom was connected with that of the Church (Psa 122:8, 9).

entries.title: Proverbs 13:10–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-18. That choice is expressed in God's words, "I will sit" or "dwell," or sit enthroned. The joy of the people springs from the blessings of His grace, conferred through the medium of the priesthood.

entries.title: Proverbs 13:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 133 Psa 133:1-3. The blessings of fraternal unity. 1-2. As the fragrant oil is refreshing, so this affords delight. The holy anointing oil for the high priest was olive oil mixed with four of the best spices (Ex 30:22, 25, 30). Its rich profusion typified the abundance of the Spirit's graces. As the copious dew, such as fell on Hermon, falls in fertilizing power on the mountains of Zion, so this unity is fruitful in good works.

entries.title: Proverbs 13:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. there-- that is, in Zion, the Church; the material Zion, blessed with enriching dews, suggests this allusion the source of the influence enjoyed by the spiritual Zion. commanded the blessing-- (Compare Psa 68:28).

entries.title: Proverbs 13:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 134 Psa 134:1-3. 1, 2. The pilgrim bands arriving at the sanctuary call on the priests, who stand in the house of the Lord-- at the time of the evening sacrifice, to unite in praising God in their name and that of the people, using appropriate gestures, to which the priests reply, pronouncing the Mosaic blessing which they alone could pronounce. A fit epilogue to the whole pilgrim-book, Psalms 120-134. by night-- the evening service (Psa 141:2), as opposed to morning (Psa 92:2). 2. Lift up your hands-- (Compare Psa 28:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 13:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. After the manner directed (Nu 6:23). out of Zion-- the Church, as His residence, and thus seat of blessings. Thus close the songs of degrees.

entries.title: Proverbs 13:24–14:0
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 135 Psa 135:1-21. A Psalm of praise, in which God's relations to His Church, His power in the natural world, and in delivering His people, are contrasted with the vanity of idols and idol-worship. 1-3. In the general call for praise, the priests, that stand in the house of the Lord, are specially mentioned.

entries.title: Proverbs 14:1–4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-7. God's choice of Israel is the first reason assigned for rendering praise; the next, His manifested greatness in creation and providence.

entries.title: Proverbs 14:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. The last plague [Ex 12:29] is cited to illustrate His "tokens and wonders."

entries.title: Proverbs 14:7–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-12. The conquest of Canaan was by God's power, not that of the people.

entries.title: Proverbs 14:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. heritage-- or, "possession." name ... memorial-- Each denote that by which God is made known.

entries.title: Proverbs 14:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. will judge-- do justice (Psa 72:2). repent himself-- change His dealings (Psa 90:13).

entries.title: Proverbs 14:12–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 14:16–18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-21. (Compare Psa 115:9-11). There we have "trust" for "bless" here.

entries.title: Proverbs 14:20–22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 136 Psa 136:1-26. The theme is the same as that of Psalm 135. God should be praised for His works of creation and providence, His deliverance and care of His people, and judgments on their enemies, and His goodness to all. The chorus to every verse is in terms of that of Psa 106:1; 118:1-4, and was perhaps used as the Amen by the people, in worship (compare 1Ch 16:36; Psa 105:45). 1-3. The divine titles denote supremacy.

entries.title: Proverbs 14:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. alone-- excluding all help.

entries.title: Proverbs 14:24–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. by wisdom-- or, "in wisdom" (Psa 104:24). made-- literally, "maker of." above the waters-- or, "higher than the waters" (Psa 24:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 14:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. Compare similar expressions (Ex 3:20; De 4:34, &c.).

entries.title: Proverbs 14:34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. overthrew-- literally, "shook off," as in Ex 14:27, as a contemptuous rejection of a reptile.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. remembered us-- or, "for us" (Psa 132:1). our low estate-- that is, captivity.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. And hath redeemed us-- or, literally, "snatched us"--alluding to the sudden deliverance effected by the overthrow of Babylon.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. To the special favors to His people is added the record of God's goodness to all His creatures (compare Mt 6:30).

entries.title: Proverbs 15:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. God of heaven-- occurs but once (Jon 1:9) before the captivity. It is used by the later writers as specially distinguishing God from idols.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 137 Psa 137:1-9. This Psalm records the mourning of the captive Israelites, and a prayer and prediction respecting the destruction of their enemies. 1. rivers of Babylon-- the name of the city used for the whole country. remembered Zion-- or, Jerusalem, as in Psa 132:13.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. upon the willows-- which may have grown there then, if not now; as the palm, which was once common, is now rare in Palestine.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. Whether the request was in curiosity or derision, the answer intimates that a compliance was incongruous with their mournful feelings (Pr 25:20).

entries.title: Proverbs 15:15–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. For joyful songs would imply forgetfulness of their desolated homes and fallen Church. The solemn imprecations on the hand and tongue, if thus forgetful, relate to the cunning or skill in playing, and the power of singing.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:17–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-9. Remember ... the children of Edom-- (Compare Psa 132:1), that is, to punish. the day of Jerusalem-- its downfall (La 4:21, 22; Ob 11-13).

entries.title: Proverbs 15:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 138 Psa 138:1-8. David thanks God for His benefits, and anticipating a wider extension of God's glory by His means, assures himself of His continued presence and faithfulness. 1. I will praise thee with my whole heart-- (Compare Psa 9:1). before the gods-- whether angels (Psa 8:5); or princes (Ex 21:6; Psa 82:6); or idols (Psa 97:7); denotes a readiness to worship the true God alone, and a contempt of all other objects of worship.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. (Compare Psa 5:7). thy word above all thy name-- that is, God's promise (2Sa 7:12-16), sustained by His mercy and truth, exceeded all other manifestations of Himself as subject of praise.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:23–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-5. That promise, as an answer to his prayers in distress, revived and strengthened his faith; and, as the basis of other revelations of the Messiah, it will be the occasion of praise by all who hear and receive it (Psa 68:29, 31; Is 4:3).

entries.title: Proverbs 15:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6-7. On this general principle of God's government (Is 2:11; 57:15; 66:2), he relies for God's favor in saving him, and overthrowing his enemies. knoweth afar off-- their ways and deserts (Psa 1:6).

entries.title: Proverbs 15:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. God will fulfil His promise.

entries.title: Proverbs 15:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 139 Psa 139:1-24. After presenting the sublime doctrines of God's omnipresence and omniscience, the Psalmist appeals to Him, avowing his innocence, his abhorrence of the wicked, and his ready submission to the closest scrutiny. Admonition to the wicked and comfort to the pious are alike implied inferences from these doctrines.

entries.title: Proverbs 16:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 140 Psa 140:1-13. The style of this Psalm resembles those of David in the former part of the book, presenting the usual complaint, prayer, and confident hope of relief. 1. evil man-- Which of David's enemies is meant is not important.

entries.title: Proverbs 16:22–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2-5. This character of the wicked, and the devices planned against the pious, correspond to Psa 10:7; 31:13; 58:4, &c.

entries.title: Proverbs 16:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 16:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. day of battle-- literally, "of armor," that is, when using it.

entries.title: Proverbs 16:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. (Compare Psa 37:12; 66:7). lest they exalt themselves-- or, they will be exalted if permitted to prosper.

entries.title: Proverbs 16:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Contrasts his head covered by God (Psa 140:7) with theirs, or (as "head" may be used for "persons") with them, covered with the results of their wicked deeds (Psa 7:16).

entries.title: Proverbs 16:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. (Compare Psa 11:6; 120:4). cast into the fire; into deep pits-- figures for utter destruction.

entries.title: Proverbs 16:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11. an evil speaker-- or, "slanderer" will not be tolerated (Psa 101:7). The last clause may be translated: "an evil (man) He (God) shall hunt," &c.

entries.title: Proverbs 16:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. (Compare Psa 9:4).

entries.title: Proverbs 16:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. After all changes, the righteous shall have cause for praise. Such shall dwell-- shall sit securely, under God's protection (Psa 21:6; 41:12).

entries.title: Proverbs 17 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 141 Psa 141:1-10. This Psalm evinces its authorship as the preceding, by its structure and the character of its contents. It is a prayer for deliverance from sins to which affliction tempted him, and from the enemies who caused it.

entries.title: Proverbs 17:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 142 Psa 142:1-7. Maschil--(See on Psa 32:1, title). When he was in the cave--either of Adullam (1Sa 22:1), or En-gedi (1Sa 24:3). This does not mean that the Psalm was composed in the cave, but that the precarious mode of life, of which his refuge in caves was a striking illustration, occasioned the complaint, which constitutes the first part of the Psalm and furnishes the reason for the prayer with which it concludes, and which, as the prominent characteristic, gives its name. 1. with my voice-- audibly, because earnestly.

entries.title: Proverbs 17:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. (Compare Psa 62:8). I poured out my complaint-- or, "a sad musing."

entries.title: Proverbs 17:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. thou knewest ... path-- The appeal is indicative of conscious innocence; knowest it to be right, and that my affliction is owing to the snares of enemies, and is not deserved (compare Psa 42:4; 61:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 17:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. Utter desolation is meant. right hand-- the place of a protector (Psa 110:5). cared for-- literally, "sought after," to do good.

entries.title: Proverbs 17:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 17:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. (Compare Psa 17:1).

entries.title: Proverbs 17:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. (Compare Psa 25:17). that I may praise-- literally, "for praising," or, "that Thy name may be praised," that is, by the righteous, who shall surround me with sympathizing joy (Psa 35:27).

entries.title: Proverbs 17:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 143 Psa 143:1-12. In structure and style, like the preceding (Psalms 104-142), this Psalm is clearly evinced to be David's. It is a prayer for pardon, and for relief from enemies; afflictions, as usual, producing confession and penitence. 1. in thy faithfulness ... and ... righteousness-- or, God's regard to the claims which He has permitted His people to make in His covenant.

entries.title: Proverbs 17:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. enter ... judgment-- deal not in strict justice. shall no ... justified-- or, "is no man justified," or "innocent" (Job 14:3; Ro 3:20).

entries.title: Proverbs 17:22–23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. The exciting reason for his prayer-- his afflictions--led to confession as just made: he now makes the complaint. as those that have been long dead-- deprived of life's comforts (compare Psa 40:15; 88:3-6).

entries.title: Proverbs 17:24–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. The distress is aggravated by the contrast of former comfort (Psa 22:3-5), for whose return he longs. a thirsty land-- which needs rain, as did his spirit God's gracious visits (Psa 28:1; 89:17).

entries.title: Proverbs 17:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. spirit faileth-- is exhausted.

entries.title: Proverbs 17:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. (Compare Psa 25:1-4; 59:16). the way ... walk-- that is, the way of safety and righteousness (Psa 142:3-6).

entries.title: Proverbs 17:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. (Compare Psa 31:15-20).

entries.title: Proverbs 18 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. (Compare Psa 5:8; 27:11). land of uprightness-- literally, "an even land" (Psa 26:12).

entries.title: Proverbs 18:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 18:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. God's mercy to His people is often wrath to His and their enemies (compare Psa 31:17). thy servant-- as chosen to be such, entitled to divine regard.

entries.title: Proverbs 18:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 144 Psa 144:1-15. David's praise of God as his all-sufficient help is enhanced by a recognition of the intrinsic worthlessness of man. Confidently imploring God's interposition against his enemies, he breaks forth into praise and joyful anticipations of the prosperity of his kingdom, when freed from vain and wicked men.

entries.title: Proverbs 18:20–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 145 Psa 145:1-21. A Psalm of praise to God for His mighty, righteous, and gracious government of all men, and of His humble and suffering people in particular. 1-2. (Compare Psa 30:1). bless thy name-- celebrate Thy perfections (Psa 5:11). God is addressed as king, alluding to His government of men.

entries.title: Proverbs 18:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. (Compare Psa 18:3; 48:1). greatness-- as displayed in His works.

entries.title: Proverbs 18:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. shall declare-- literally, "they shall declare," that is, all generations.

entries.title: Proverbs 18:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. I will speak-- or, "muse" (Psa 77:12; 119:15). thy wondrous works-- or, "words of thy wonders," that is, which described them (Psa 105:27, Margin).

entries.title: Proverbs 19 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. terrible acts-- which produce dread or fear.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. memory-- (Psa 6:5), remembrance, or what causes to be remembered. righteousness-- as in Psa 143:1, goodness according to covenant engagement.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8-9. (Compare Psa 103:8; 111:4). over all, &c.-- rests on all His works.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. bless-- as in Psa 145:1, to praise with reverence, more than merely to praise.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:5–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. The declaration of God's glory is for the extension of His knowledge and perfections in the world.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 19:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 19:9–10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-16. eyes of ... thee-- or, look with expecting faith (Psa 104:27, 28).

entries.title: Proverbs 19:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. holy ... works-- literally, "merciful" or "kind, goodness" (Psa 144:2) is the corresponding noun. righteous-- in a similar relation of meaning to "righteousness" (Psa 145:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 19:12–13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 19:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. Those who fear Him (Psa 145:19) are those who are here said to love Him.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. (Compare Psa 33:21). all flesh-- (Psa 65:2). The Psalm ends, as it began, with ascriptions of praise, in which the pious will ever delight to join.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 146 Psa 146:1-10. An exhortation to praise God, who, by the gracious and faithful exercise of His power in goodness to the needy, is alone worthy of implicit trust.

entries.title: Proverbs 19:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 147 Psa 147:1-20. This and the remaining Psalms have been represented as specially designed to celebrate the rebuilding of Jerusalem (compare Ne 6:16; 12:27). They all open and close with the stirring call for praise. This one specially declares God's providential care towards all creatures, and particularly His people. 1. (Compare Psa 92:1; 135:3).

entries.title: Proverbs 19:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. (Compare Psa 107:3; Is 11:12).

entries.title: Proverbs 20 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. Though applicable to the captive Israelites, this is a general and precious truth. wounds-- (Compare Margin).

entries.title: Proverbs 20:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-5. God's power in nature (Is 40:26-28, and often) is presented as a pledge of His power to help His people. telleth ... stars-- what no man can do (Ge 15:5).

entries.title: Proverbs 20:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. That power is put forth for the good of the meek and suffering pious, and confusion of the wicked (Psa 146:8, 9).

entries.title: Proverbs 20:4–6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-9. His providence supplies bountifully the wild animals in their mountain homes. Sing ... Lord-- literally, "Answer the Lord," that is, in grateful praise to His goodness, thus declared in His acts.

entries.title: Proverbs 20:7–8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. The advantages afforded, as in war by the strength of the horse or the agility of man, do not incline God to favor any; but those who fear and, of course, trust Him, will obtain His approbation and aid.

entries.title: Proverbs 20:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. strengthened ... gates-- or, means of defense against invaders,

entries.title: Proverbs 20:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. maketh ... borders-- or, territories (Ge 23:17; Is 54:12). filleth thee, &c.-- (Compare Margin).

entries.title: Proverbs 20:12–15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-18. God's Word, as a swift messenger, executes His purpose, for with Him to command is to perform (Ge 1:3; Psa 33:9), and He brings about the wonders of providence as easily as men cast crumbs.

entries.title: Proverbs 20:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-20. This mighty ruler and benefactor of heaven and earth is such especially to His chosen people, to whom alone (De 4:32-34) He has made known His will, while others have been left in darkness. Therefore unite in the great hallelujah.

entries.title: Proverbs 20:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 148 Psa 148:1-14. The scope of this Psalm is the same as that of the preceding. 1. heavens [and] heights-- are synonymous.

entries.title: Proverbs 20:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. hosts-- (compare Psa 103:21).

entries.title: Proverbs 20:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. heavens of heavens-- the very highest. waters-- clouds, resting above the visible heavens (compare Ge 1:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 20:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. praise the name-- as representing His perfections. he commanded-- "He" is emphatic, ascribing creation to God alone.

entries.title: Proverbs 20:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. The perpetuity of the frame of nature is, of course, subject to Him who formed it. a decree ... pass-- His ordinances respecting them shall not change (Jr 36:31), or perish (Job 34:20; Psa 37:36).

entries.title: Proverbs 20:25–28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-10. The call on the earth, as opposed to heaven, includes seas or depths, whose inhabitants the dragon, as one of the largest (on leviathan, see on Psa 104:26), is selected to represent. The most destructive and ungovernable agents of inanimate nature are introduced.

entries.title: Proverbs 20:29–30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. Next all rational beings, from the highest in rank to little children. princes-- or, military leaders.

entries.title: Proverbs 21 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. Let them-- all mentioned. excellent-- or, exalted (Is 12:4). his glory-- majesty (Psa 45:3). above the earth and heaven-- Their united splendors fail to match His.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. exalteth the horn-- established power (Psa 75:5, 6). praise of-- or literally, "for" his saints-- that is, occasions for them to praise Him. They are further described as "His people," and "near unto Him," sustaining by covenanted care a peculiarly intimate relation.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
