entries.content: PSALM 149 Psa 149:1-9. This Psalm sustains a close connection with the foregoing. The chosen people are exhorted to praise God, in view of past favors, and also future victories over enemies, of which they are impliedly assured. 1. (Compare Psa 96:1).

entries.title: Proverbs 21:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. God had signalized His relation as a sovereign, in restoring them to their land.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. in the dance-- (Psa 30:11). The dance is connected with other terms, expressive of the great joy of the occasion. The word may be rendered "lute," to which the other instruments are joined. sing praises-- or, sing and play.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. taketh pleasure-- literally, "accepts," alluding to acceptance of propitiatory offerings (compare Psa 147:11). beautify, &c.-- adorn the humble with faith, hope, joy, and peace.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. in glory-- the honorable condition to which they are raised. upon their beds-- once a place of mourning (Psa 6:6).

entries.title: Proverbs 21:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. high praises-- or, "deeds." They shall go forth as religious warriors, as once religious laborers (Ne 4:17).

entries.title: Proverbs 21:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The destruction of the incorrigibly wicked attends the propagation of God's truth, so that the military successes of the Jews, after the captivity, typified the triumphs of the Gospel.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. the judgment written-- either in God's decrees, or perhaps as in De 32:41-43. this honour-- that is, to be thus employed, will be an honorable service, to be assigned his saints-- or, godly ones (Psa 16:3).

entries.title: Proverbs 21:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: PSALM 150 Psa 150:1-6. This is a suitable doxology for the whole book, reciting the "place, theme, mode, and extent of God's high praise." 1. in his sanctuary-- on earth. firmament of his power-- which illustrates His power.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. mighty acts-- (Psa 145:4). excellent greatness-- or, abundance of greatness.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:15–16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3-4. trumpet-- used to call religious assemblies;

entries.title: Proverbs 21:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. cymbals-- suited to loud praise (Ne 12:27).

entries.title: Proverbs 21:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Living voices shall take up the failing sounds of dead instruments, and as they cease on earth, those of intelligent ransomed spirits and holy angels, as with the sound of mighty thunders, will prolong eternally the praise, saying: "Alleluia! Salvation, and Glory, and Honor, and Power, unto the Lord our God;" "Alleluia! for the Lord God omnipotent reigneth." Amen!

entries.title: Proverbs 21:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: THE BOOK OF PROVERBS.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:21–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: INTRODUCTION I. The Nature and Use of Proverbs.-- A proverb is a pithy sentence, concisely expressing some well-established truth susceptible of various illustrations and applications. The word is of Latin derivation, literally meaning for a word, speech, or discourse; that is, one expression for many. The Hebrew word for "proverb" (mashal) means a "comparison." Many suppose it was used, because the form or matter of the proverb, or both, involved the idea of comparison. Most of the proverbs are in couplets or triplets, or some modifications of them, the members of which correspond in structure and length, as if arranged to be compared one with another. They illustrate the varieties of parallelism, a distinguishing feature of Hebrew poetry. Compare Introduction to Poetical Books. Many also clearly involve the idea of comparison in the sentiments expressed (compare Pr 12:1-10; 25:10-15; 26:1-9). Sometimes, however, the designed omission of one member of the comparison, exercising the reader's sagacity or study for its supply, presents the proverb as a "riddle" or "dark saying" (compare Pr 30:15-33; 1:6; Psa 49:4). The sententious form of expression, which thus became a marked feature of the proverbial style, was also adopted for continuous discourse, even when not always preserving traces of comparison, either in form or matter (compare Pr 1:1-9:18). In Eze 17:1; 24:3, we find the same word properly translated "parable," to designate an illustrative discourse. Then the Greek translators have used a word, parabola ("parable"), which the gospel writers (except John) employ for our Lord's discourses of the same character, and which also seems to involve the idea of comparison, though that may not be its primary meaning. It might seem, therefore, that the proverbial and parabolic styles of writing were originally and essentially the same. The proverb is a "concentrated parable, and the parable an extension of the proverb by a full illustration." The proverb is thus the moral or theme of a parable, which sometimes precedes it, as in Mt 19:30 (compare Pr 20:1); or succeeds it, as in Mt 22:1-16; Lu 15:1-10. The style being poetical, and adapted to the expression of a high order of poetical sentiment, such as prophecy, we find the same term used to designate such compositions (compare Nu 23:7; Mi 2:4; Ha 2:6). Though the Hebrews used the same term for proverb and parable, the Greek employs two, though the sacred writers have not always appeared to recognize a distinction. The term for proverb is, paroimia, which the Greek translators employ for the title of this book, evidently with special reference to the later definition of a proverb, as a trite, sententious form of speech, which appears to be the best meaning of the term. John uses the same term to designate our Saviour's instructions, in view of their characteristic obscurity (compare Pr 16:25-29, Greek), and even for his illustrative discourses (Pr 10:6), whose sense was not at once obvious to all his hearers. This form of instruction was well adapted to aid the learner. The parallel structure of sentences, the repetition, contrast, or comparison of thought, were all calculated to facilitate the efforts of memory; and precepts of practical wisdom which, extended into logical discourses, might have failed to make abiding impressions by reason of their length or complicated character, were thus compressed into pithy, and, for the most part, very plain statements. Such a mode of instruction has distinguished the written or traditional literature of all nations, and was, and still is, peculiarly current in the East. In this book, however, we are supplied with a proverbial wisdom commended by the seal of divine inspiration. God has condescended to become our teacher on the practical affairs belonging to all the relations of life. He has adapted His instruction to the plain and unlettered, and presented, in this striking and impressive method, the great principles of duty to Him and to our fellow men. To the prime motive of all right conduct, the fear of God, are added all lawful and subordinate incentives, such as honor, interest, love, fear, and natural affection. Besides the terror excited by an apprehension of God's justly provoked judgments, we are warned against evil-doing by the exhibition of the inevitable temporal results of impiety, injustice, profligacy, idleness, laziness, indolence, drunkenness, and debauchery. To the rewards of true piety which follow in eternity, are promised the peace, security, love, and approbation of the good, and the comforts of a clear conscience, which render this life truly happy. II. Inspiration and Authorship.-- With no important exception, Jewish and Christian writers have received this book as the inspired production of Solomon. It is the first book of the Bible prefaced by the name of the author. The New Testament abounds with citations from the Proverbs. Its intrinsic excellence commends it to us as the production of a higher authority than the apocryphal writings, such as Wisdom or Ecclesiasticus. Solomon lived five hundred years before the "seven wise men" of Greece, and seven hundred before the age of Socrates, Plato, and Aristotle. It is thus very evident, whatever theory of his sources of knowledge be adopted, that he did not draw upon any heathen repositories with which we are acquainted. It is far more probable, that by the various migrations, captivities, and dispersions of the Jews, heathen philosophers drew from this inspired fountain many of those streams which continue to refresh mankind amid the otherwise barren and parched deserts of profane literature. As, however, the Psalms are ascribed to David, because he was the leading author, so the ascription of this book to Solomon is entirely consistent with the titles of the thirtieth and thirty-first chapters, which assign those chapters to Agur and Lemuel respectively. Of these persons we know nothing. This is not the place for discussing the various speculations respecting them. By a slight change of reading some propose to translate Pr 30:1: "The words of Agur, the son of her who was obeyed Massa," that is, "the queen of Massa"; and Pr 31:1: "The words of Lemuel, king of Massa"; but to this the earliest versions are contradictory, and nothing other than the strongest exegetical necessity ought to be allowed to justify a departure from a well-established reading and version when nothing useful to our knowledge is gained. It is better to confess ignorance than indulge in useless conjectures. It is probable that out of the "three thousand proverbs" (1Ki 4:32) which Solomon spoke, he selected and edited Pr 1:1-24:34 during his life. Pr 25:1-29:27 were also of his production, and copied out in the days of Hezekiah, by his "men," perhaps the prophets Isaiah, Hosea, and Micah. Such a work was evidently in the spirit of this pious monarch, who set his heart so fully on a reformation of God's worship. Learned men have endeavored to establish the theory that Solomon himself was only a collector; or that the other parts of the book, as these chapters, were also selections by later hands; but the reasons adduced to maintain these views have never appeared so satisfactory as to change the usual opinions on the subject, which have the sanction of the most ancient and reliable authorities. III. Divisions of the Book.-- Such a work is, of course, not susceptible of any logical analysis. There are, however, some well-defined marks of division, so that very generally the book is divided into five or six parts. 1. The first contains nine chapters, in which are discussed and enforced by illustration, admonition, and encouragement the principles and blessings of wisdom, and the pernicious schemes and practices of sinful persons. These chapters are introductory. With few specimens of the proper proverb, they are distinguished by its conciseness and terseness. The sentences follow very strictly the form of parallelism, and generally of the synonymous species, only forty of the synthetic and four (Pr 3:32-35) of the antithetic appearing. The style is ornate, the figures bolder and fuller, and the illustrations more striking and extended. 2. The antithetic and synthetic parallelism to the exclusion of the synonymous distinguish Pr 10:1-22:16, and the verses are entirely unconnected, each containing a complete sense in itself. 3. Pr 22:16-24:34 present a series of admonitions as if addressed to a pupil, and generally each topic occupies two or more verses. 4. Pr 25:1-29:27 are entitled to be regarded as a distinct portion, for the reason given above as to its origin. The style is very much mixed; of the peculiarities, compare parts two and three. 5. Pr 30:1-33 is peculiar not only for its authorship, but as a specimen of the kind of proverb which has been described as "dark sayings" or "riddles." 6. To a few pregnant but concise admonitions, suitable for a king, is added a most inimitable portraiture of female character. In both parts five and six the distinctive peculiarity of the original proverbial style gives place to the modifications already mentioned as marking a later composition, though both retain the concise and nervous method of stating truth, equally valuable for its deep impression and permanent retention by the memory. CHAPTER 1 Pr 1:1-33. After the title the writer defines the design and nature of the instructions of the book. He paternally invites attention to those instructions and warns his readers against the enticements of the wicked. In a beautiful personification, wisdom is then introduced in a most solemn and impressive manner, publicly inviting men to receive its teachings, warning those who reject, and encouraging those who accept, the proffered instructions. 1-4. (See Introduction, Part I).

entries.title: Proverbs 21:25–26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5-6. Such writings the wise, who pursue right ends by right means, will value. learning-- not the act, but matter of it. wise counsels-- or the art and principles of governing.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. The fear of the Lord-- the principle of true piety (compare Pr 2:5; 14:26, 27; Job 28:28; Psa 34:11; 111:10; Ac 9:31). beginning-- first part, foundation. fools-- the stupid and indifferent to God's character and government; hence the wicked.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. My son-- This paternal form denotes a tender regard for the reader. Filial sentiments rank next to piety towards God, and ensure most distinguished rewards (compare Pr 6:20; Ep 6:2, 3).

entries.title: Proverbs 21:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. On the figures of Pr 1:9, compare Ge 41:42; So 1:10; 4:9.

entries.title: Proverbs 21:30–22:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-19. A solemn warning against temptation. entice-- literally, "open the way." consent ... not-- Sin is in consenting or yielding to temptation, not in being tempted.

entries.title: Proverbs 22:8–21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-33. Some interpreters regard this address as the language of the Son of God under the name of Wisdom (compare Lu 11:49). Others think that wisdom, as the divine attribute specially employed in acts of counsel and admonition, is here personified, and represents God. In either case the address is a most solemn and divine admonition, whose matter and spirit are eminently evangelical and impressive (see on Pr 8:1). Wisdom-- literally, "Wisdoms," the plural used either because of the unusual sense, or as indicative of the great excellency of wisdom (compare Pr 9:1). streets-- or most public places, not secretly.

entries.title: Proverbs 22:23–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 2 Pr 2:1-22. Men are invited to seek wisdom because it teaches those principles by which they may obtain God's guidance and avoid the society and influence of the wicked, whose pernicious courses are described. 1-5. Diligence in hearing and praying for instruction must be used to secure the great principle of godliness, the fear of God. hide ... with thee-- lay up in store (compare Pr 7:1).

entries.title: Proverbs 22:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. For-- God is ready (Jas 1:5; 4:8). out of his mouth-- by revelation from Him.

entries.title: Proverbs 22:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. sound wisdom-- literally, "substance," opposed to what is fictitious. According to the context, this may be assistance, as here corresponding with buckler-- or safety, or wisdom, which procures it (compare Pr 3:21; 8:14; 18:1; Job 6:13; 12:13). layeth up-- provides, ever ready.

entries.title: Proverbs 23 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. keepeth ... way-- God defends the right way, and those in it. saints-- objects of favor (compare Psa 4:3, &c.). He guides and guards them.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. Then-- emphatic, in such a case. righteousness ... path-- all parts of duty to God and man.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:2–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. Idea of Pr 2:9, amplified; on terms, compare Pr 2:2 and Pr 2:4.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:4–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12-15. To deliver-- as from great danger (Pr 6:5). way ... man-- (Psa 1:1). froward things-- perversity (Pr 6:14; 23:23), what is opposed to truth.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:8–11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-19. Deliverance from another danger. the strange woman-- This term is often used for harlot, or loose woman (Jud 11:1, 2), married (Pr 7:5, 19) or not (1Ki 11:1), so called, because such were, perhaps at first, foreigners, though "strange" may also denote whatever is opposed to right or proper, as "strange fire" (Nu 3:4); "strange incense" (Ex 30:9). flattereth-- literally, "smooths." her words-- (Psa 5:9).

entries.title: Proverbs 23:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. That ... way of good-- that is, Such is the object of these warnings.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:13–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 23:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 3 Pr 3:1-35. The study of truth commended. God must be feared, honored, and trusted, and filial submission, under chastisement, exhibited. The excellence of wisdom urged and illustrated by its place in the divine counsels. Piety enforced by a contrast of the destiny of the righteous and the wicked. 1. law [and] commandments-- all divine instructions (see on Psa 119:1 and Psa 119:4). let thine heart keep-- or sincerely observe (Pr 4:13; 5:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 23:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. length ... life-- often promised as blessings (Psa 21:4; 91:16). peace-- includes prosperity (Psa 125:5). add-- abound to thee.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. mercy and truth-- God's faithfulness to His promises is often expressed by these terms (Psa 25:10; 57:3). As attributes of men, they express integrity in a wide sense (Pr 16:6; 20:28). bind ... write ... heart-- outwardly adorn and inwardly govern motives.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. favour-- grace, amiability (Pr 22:11; Psa 45:2); united with this, a good understanding-- (Compare Margin), a discrimination, which secures success. in the sight ... man-- such as God and man approve.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Trust ... heart-- This is the center and marrow of true wisdom (Pr 22:19; 28:25). The positive duty has its corresponding negation in the admonition against self-confidence.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. ways-- (Psa 1:1). acknowledge-- by seeking His wise aid (Pr 16:3; Psa 37:5; Jr 9:23, 24). direct-- literally, "make plain" (compare He 12:13).

entries.title: Proverbs 23:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. (Compare Pr 27:2; Ro 12:16). fear ... evil-- reverentially regarding His law.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. It-- This conduct. health-- (Compare Margin). to thy navel-- for all the organs of nourishment. marrow-- (Compare Margin). bones-- frame of body. True piety promotes bodily health.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:24–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 23:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. The true intent of afflictions considered; they do not contradict the assertion of the blessed state of the pious (Job 5:17; He 12:5, 6).

entries.title: Proverbs 23:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. findeth-- literally, "reaches," or "obtains by seeking." getteth-- literally, "draws out," as metals by digging.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:29–30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14-15. The figure of Pr 3:13 carried out. it-- that is, wisdom. merchandise of silver-- acquisition by trading. fine gold-- dug gold, solid as a nugget.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:31–32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. Wisdom personified as bringing the best blessings (compare Mt 6:33; 1Ti 4:8).

entries.title: Proverbs 23:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. Wisdom allegorized as a tree of life-- (Ge 2:9; 3:22) whose fruit preserves life, gives all that makes living a blessing.

entries.title: Proverbs 23:34–35
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19-20. The place of wisdom in the economy of creation and providence commends it to men, who, in proportion to their finite powers, may possess this invaluable attribute, and are thus encouraged by the divine example of its use to seek its possession.

entries.title: Proverbs 24 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. sound wisdom-- (compare Pr 2:7). let ... eyes-- that is, these words of instruction.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:1–3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-24. assign reasons in their value for happiness and ornament, guidance and support in dangers, both when waking and sleeping.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. Be not-- or, "You shall not be." sudden fear-- what causes it (Pr 1:27), any unlooked-for evil (Psa 46:3; 91:12; 1Pe 3:14). desolation-- (Pr 1:27).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. The reason; such as are objects of God's favor. be thy confidence-- literally, "in thy confidence," in the source of thy strength (compare Na 3:9, for the same construction, Hebrew).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:6–7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27-28. Promptly fulfil all obligations both of justice and charity (compare Jas 2:15, 16).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 29-30. Do not abuse confidence and avoid litigation.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 31. oppressor-- or man of mischief. The destiny of successful evildoers warns against desiring their lot (Psa 37:1, 2, 35, 36).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:11–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32-35. Reasons for the warning. froward-- (Pr 2:15). secret ... righteous-- in their communion (Am 3:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 4 Pr 4:1-27. To an earnest call for attention to his teachings, the writer adds a commendation of wisdom, preceded and enforced by the counsels of his father and teacher. To this he adds a caution (against the devices of the wicked), and a series of exhortations to docility, integrity, and uprightness. 1-2. (Compare Pr 1:8). to know-- in order to know. doctrine-- the matter of learning (Pr 1:5), such as he had received (La 3:1).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. father's son-- emphatic, a son specially regarded, and so called tender, as an object of special care (compare 1Ch 22:7; 29:1); an idea further expressed by only beloved-- or, "as an only son" (Ge 22:2), though he had brothers (see on 1Ch 3:5).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. He taught-- or directed me. retain-- as well as receive. keep ... and live-- observe, that you may live (Pr 7:2).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. Get-- as a possession not to be given up. neither decline-- that is, from obeying my word.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Not only accept but love wisdom, who will keep thee from evil, and evil from thee.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. (Compare Job 28:28). getting-- or possession; a desire for wisdom is wise.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. As you highly esteem her, she will raise you to honor. embrace her-- with fond affection.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. ornament-- such as the chaplet or wreath of conquerors. deliver-- (Compare Ge 14:20). The allusion to a shield, contained in the Hebrew, suggests protection as well as honor (compare Pr 4:6).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 24:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. way of wisdom-- which it prescribes. led thee-- literally, "caused thee to tread," as a path (Psa 107:7). not be straitened-- have ample room (Psa 18:36).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. (Compare Pr 3:18). The figure of laying hold with the hand suggests earnest effort.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. (Compare Psa 1:1). Avoid all temptations to the beginning of evil.

entries.title: Proverbs 24:31–32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16-17. The reason is found in the character of sinners, whose zeal to do evil is forcibly depicted (Pr 6:4; Psa 36:5). They live by flagrant vices (Pr 1:13). Some prefer to render, "Their bread is wickedness, their drink violence" (compare Job 15:16; 34:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 24:33–34
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18-19. As shining light increases from twilight to noonday splendor, so the course of the just increases in purity, but that of the wicked is as thickest darkness, in which one knows not on what he stumbles.

entries.title: Proverbs 25 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 25:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. with all diligence-- or, "above," or "more than all," custody (compare Margin), all that is kept (compare Eze 38:7), because the heart is the depository of all wisdom and the source of whatever affects life and character (Mt 12:35; 15:19).

entries.title: Proverbs 25:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. a froward mouth-- that is, a mouth, or words of ill nature. The Hebrew word differs from that used (Pr 2:15; 3:32). perverse-- or, "quarreling." lips-- or, "words."

entries.title: Proverbs 25:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. Let ... before thee-- that is, pursue a sincere and direct purpose, avoiding temptations.

entries.title: Proverbs 25:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. Ponder-- Consider well; a wise course results from wise forethought.

entries.title: Proverbs 25:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. (Compare Pr 4:25). Avoid all by-paths of evil (De 2:27; 17:11). A life of integrity requires attention to heart, speech, eyes, and conduct.

entries.title: Proverbs 25:9

entries.content: CHAPTER 5 Pr 5:1-23. A warning against the seductive arts of wicked women, enforced by considering the advantages of chastity, and the miserable end of the wicked. 1. This connection of wisdom and understanding is frequent (Pr 2:2; 3:7); the first denotes the use of wise means for wise ends; the other, the exercise of a proper discrimination in their discovery.

entries.title: Proverbs 25:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. regard-- or, "observe." keep-- preserve constantly.

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.content: 4. her end-- literally, "her future," in sense of reward, what follows (compare Psa 37:37; 73:17). Its nature is evinced by the use of figures, opposite those of Pr 5:3. The physical and moral suffering of the deluded profligate are notoriously terrible.

entries.title: Proverbs 25:13

entries.content: 6. her ways ... know-- Some prefer, "that she may not ponder the path of life," &c.; but perhaps a better sense is, "her ways are varied, so as to prevent your knowledge of her true character, and so of true happiness."

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15-20. By figures, in which well, cistern, and fountain [Pr 5:15, 18] represent the wife, and rivers of waters [Pr 5:16] the children, men are exhorted to constancy and satisfaction in lawful conjugal enjoyments. In Pr 5:16, fountains (in the plural) rather denote the produce or waters of a spring, literally, "what is from a spring," and corresponds with "rivers of waters."

entries.content: CHAPTER 6 Pr 6:1-35. After admonitions against suretyship and sloth (compare Pr 6:6-8), the character and fate of the wicked generally are set forth, and the writer (Pr 6:20-35) resumes the warnings against incontinence, pointing out its certain and terrible results. This train of thought seems to intimate the kindred of these vices. 1-2. if-- The condition extends through both verses. be surety-- art pledged. stricken ... hand-- bargained (compare Job 17:3). with a stranger-- that is, for a friend (compare Pr 11:15; 17:18).

entries.title: Proverbs 26:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 26:7–8

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 26:16–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 26:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Suddenness aggravates evil (compare Pr 6:11; 29:1). calamity-- literally, "a crushing weight." broken-- shivered as a potter's vessel; utterly destroyed (Psa 2:9).

entries.title: Proverbs 26:19–22

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 11-12. loud-- or, "noisy," "bustling." stubborn-- not submissive. without ... streets, ... corner-- (Compare 1Ti 5:13; Tit 2:5).

entries.title: Proverbs 27:23–25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13-15. The preparations for a feast do not necessarily imply peculiar religious professions. The offerer retained part of the victim for a feast (Le 3:9, &c.). This feast she professes was prepared for him whom she boldly addresses as one sought specially to partake of it.

entries.title: Proverbs 27:26–27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 28 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 28:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. caused ... yield-- or, "inclines." flattering-- (Compare Pr 5:3). forced him-- by persuasion overcoming his scruples.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. straightway-- quickly, either as ignorant of danger, or incapable of resistance.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. Till-- He is now caught (Pr 6:26).

entries.title: Proverbs 28:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. The inferential admonition is followed (Pr 7:26, 27), by a more general allegation of the evils of this vice.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:8–9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26-27. Even the mightiest fail to resist her deathly allurements.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:11–14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 8 Pr 8:1-36. Contrasted with sensual allurements are the advantages of divine wisdom, which publicly invites men, offers the best principles of life, and the most valuable benefits resulting from receiving her counsels. Her relation to the divine plans and acts is introduced, as in Pr 3:19, 20, though more fully, to commend her desirableness for men, and the whole is closed by an assurance that those finding her find God's favor, and those neglecting ruin themselves. Many regard the passage as a description of the Son of God by the title, Wisdom, which the older Jews used (and by which He is called in Lu 11:49), as Joh 1:1, &c., describes Him by that of Logos, the Word. But the passage may be taken as a personification of wisdom: for, (1) Though described as with God, wisdom is not asserted to be God. (2) The use of personal attributes is equally consistent with a personification, as with the description of a real person. (3) The personal pronouns used accord with the gender (feminine) of wisdom constantly, and are never changed to that of the person meant, as sometimes occurs in a corresponding use of spirit, which is neuter in Greek, but to which masculine pronouns are often applied (Joh 16:14), when the acts of the Holy Spirit are described. (4) Such a personification is agreeable to the style of this book (compare Pr 1:20; 3:16, 17; 4:8; 6:20-22; 9:1-4), whereas no prophetical or other allusions to the Saviour or the new dispensation are found among the quotations of this book in the New Testament, and unless this be such, none exist. (5) Nothing is lost as to the importance of this passage, which still remains a most ornate and also solemn and impressive teaching of inspiration on the value of wisdom. 1-4. The publicity and universality of the call contrast with the secrecy and intrigues of the wicked (Pr 7:8, &c.).

entries.title: Proverbs 28:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. wisdom-- literally, "subtilty" in a good sense, or, "prudence." fools-- as Pr 1:22.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 28:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. For ... truth-- literally, "My palate shall meditate," or (as Orientals did) "mutter," my thoughts expressed only to myself are truth. wickedness-- specially falsehood, as opposed to truth.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. in righteousness-- or, "righteous" (Psa 9:8,11:7). froward-- literally, "twisted," or contradictory, that is, to truth.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. plain ... understandeth-- easily seen by those who apply their minds. that find-- implying search.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. not silver-- preferable to it, so last clause implies comparison.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 28:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. prudence-- as in Pr 8:5. The connection of "wisdom" and "prudence" is that of the dictates of sound wisdom and its application. find ... inventions-- or, "devices," "discreet ways" (Pr 1:4).

entries.title: Proverbs 28:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. For such is the effect of the fear of God, by which hatred to evil preserves from it. froward mouth-- or, "speech" (Pr 2:12; 6:14).

entries.title: Proverbs 28:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. It also gives the elements of good character in counsel. sound wisdom-- (Pr 2:7). I ... strength-- or, "As for me, understanding is strength to me," the source of power (Ec 9:16); good judgment gives more efficiency to actions;

entries.title: Proverbs 28:25–26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. early-- or, "diligently," which may include the usual sense of early in life.

entries.title: Proverbs 28:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. durable riches ... righteousness-- Such are the "riches," enduring sources of happiness in moral possessions (compare Pr 3:16).

entries.title: Proverbs 29 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 29:1–2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20-21. The courses in which wisdom leads conduct to a true present prosperity (Pr 23:5).

entries.title: Proverbs 29:3–12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22-31. Strictly, God's attributes are part of Himself. Yet, to the poetical structure of the whole passage, this commendation of wisdom is entirely consonant. In order of time all His attributes are coincident and eternal as Himself. But to set forth the importance of wisdom as devising the products of benevolence and power, it is here assigned a precedence. As it has such in divine, so should it be desired in human, affairs (compare Pr 3:19). possessed-- or, "created"; in either sense, the idea of precedence. in the beginning-- or simply, "beginning," in apposition with "me." before ... of old-- preceding the most ancient deeds.

entries.title: Proverbs 29:13–17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32-36. Such an attribute men are urged to seek.

entries.title: Proverbs 29:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 9 Pr 9:1-18. The commendation of wisdom is continued, under the figure of a liberal host, and its provisions under that of a feast (compare Lu 14:16-24). The character of those who are invited is followed by a contrasted description of the rejectors of good counsel; and with the invitations of wisdom are contrasted the allurement of the wicked woman. 1. house-- (compare Pr 8:34). her-- or, "its" (the house). seven pillars-- the number seven for many, or a sufficiency (Pr 6:31).

entries.title: Proverbs 29:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. mingled-- to enhance the flavor (Pr 23:30; Is 5:22). furnished-- literally, "set out," "arranged."

entries.title: Proverbs 29:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. maidens-- servants to invite (compare Psa 68:11; Is 40:9). highest places-- ridges of heights, conspicuous places.

entries.title: Proverbs 29:22–24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4-6. (Compare Pr 1:4; 6:32). Wisdom not only supplies right but forbids wrong principles.

entries.title: Proverbs 29:25–26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7-8. shame-- (Compare Pr 3:35). a blot-- or, "stain on character." Both terms denote the evil done by others to one whose faithfulness secures a wise man's love.

entries.title: Proverbs 29:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. The more a wise man learns, the more he loves wisdom.

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 30:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. on a seat-- literally, "throne," takes a prominent place, impudently and haughtily.

entries.content: 15-16. to allure those who are right-minded, and who are addressed as in Pr 9:4, as simple-- that is, easily led (Pr 1:4) and unsettled, though willing to do right.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. The language of a proverb, meaning that forbidden delights are sweet and pleasant, as fruits of risk and danger.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:8
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 30:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 10 Pr 10:1-32. Here begins the second part of the book, Pr 10:1-22:16, which, with the third, Pr 22:16-25:28, contains series of proverbs whose sense is complete in one or two verses, and which, having no logical connection, admit of no analysis. The parallelisms of Pr 10:1-15:33 are mostly antithetic; and those of Pr 16:1-22:16, synthetic. The evidences of art in the structure are very clear, and indicate, probably, a purpose of facilitating the labor of memorizing. 1. wise [and] foolish-- as they follow or reject the precepts of wisdom. maketh ... father-- or, "gladdens a father." heaviness-- or, "grief."

entries.title: Proverbs 30:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Treasures ... nothing-- that is, Ill-gotten gains give no true happiness (compare Pr 4:17; Mt 6:19). righteousness-- especially beneficence (Psa 112:9). death-- the greatest of all evils.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. (Compare Psa 37:16-20). The last clause is better: "He will repel the greedy desires of the wicked."

entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. slack-- literally, "deceitful," failing of its purpose (compare Ho 7:16). maketh rich-- (compare Pr 10:22).

entries.title: Proverbs 30:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 30:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. Blessings-- literally, "Praises." The last clause is better: "The mouth of the wicked covereth (or concealeth) violence (or mischievous devices)" to be executed in due time (Psa 5:9; 10:7; Ro 3:14), and hence has no praises (compare Pr 10:11).

entries.title: Proverbs 30:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. blessed-- literally, "for a blessing," or praise. shall rot-- literally, "be worm-eaten," useless and disgusting.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. wise, &c.-- (compare Pr 9:8, 9, 16), opposed to prating fool-- or, "fool of lips of wicked language." fall-- headlong, suddenly.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:18
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. perverteth his ways-- acts deceitfully. known-- discovered and punished.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10. Two vices contrasted; hypocrisy, or insinuating evil against one (Pr 6:13; Psa 35:19), and rashness of speech. In each case, the results are on the evildoers.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown

entries.title: Proverbs 30:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. strifes-- or, "litigations." covereth-- by forgiveness and forbearance.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. In the lips ... found-- hence, not beaten, as the wicked-speaking fool. void of understanding-- (Pr 6:32; 7:7).

entries.title: Proverbs 30:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. lay up knowledge-- that is, as treasures for good use. mouth ... destruction-- or, "as to the mouth," &c., destruction is near; they expose themselves to evil by prating.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. Both by trusting in "uncertain riches" (1Ti 6:17), or by the evils of poverty (Pr 30:9), men, not fearing God, fall into dangers.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. The industry of the righteous is alone truly successful, while the earnings of the wicked tempt and lead to sin.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. keepeth-- observes (Pr 3:18; 4:22). refuseth-- or, "turns from reproof," which might direct him aright.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. Both vices must one day be known and punished, and hence their folly.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. Much speech involves risk of sin; hence the wisdom of restraining the tongue (Psa 39:1; Jas 1:26).

entries.title: Proverbs 30:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. Right speech is the fruit of a good heart, but the wicked show theirs to be useless.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. Fools not only fail to benefit others, as do the righteous, but procure their own ruin (compare Pr 10:11, 17; Ho 4:6).

entries.title: Proverbs 30:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. it maketh, &c.-- "it" is emphatic. Riches from God are without the sorrow of ill-gotten wealth (compare Ec 2:21-23; 1Ti 6:9, 10, 17).

entries.title: Proverbs 30:32
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. Sin is the pleasure of the wicked; wisdom that of the good.

entries.title: Proverbs 30:33
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 24. it-- the very thing. The wicked get dreaded evil; the righteous, desired good.

entries.title: Proverbs 31 — Chapter Introduction
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 25. (Compare Psa 1:4; 37:9, 10, 36). righteous ... foundation-- well laid and firm (Mt 7:24, 25).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:1
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 26. that is, causes vexation.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:2
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 27. (Compare Pr 9:11; Psa 55:23).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:3
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 28. gladness-- in confidence of realizing it. expectation ... perish-- in disappointment.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:4
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 29. The way, &c.-- that is, God's providence sustains the righteous and overthrows the wicked (Ho 14:9).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:5
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 30. (Compare Pr 12:3; Psa 37:9-11; 102:28). earth-- or, "land of promise."

entries.title: Proverbs 31:6
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 31. bringeth forth-- literally, "germinates" as a plant. froward-- (Compare Pr 2:12, 14). cut off-- as an unproductive plant.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:7
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 32. know-- regard and provide for (Psa 1:6). frowardness-- all kinds of deceit and ill-nature. The word is plural.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:9
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: CHAPTER 11 Pr 11:1-31. 1. (Compare Margin). The Hebrews used stones for weights. just-- complete in measure.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:10
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 2. Self-conceit is unteachable; the humble grow wise (compare Pr 16:18; 18:12).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:11
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 3. guide-- to lead, as a shepherd (Pr 6:7; Psa 78:52). perverseness-- ill-nature. destroy-- with violence.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:12
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 4. (Compare Pr 10:2). wrath-- that is, of God.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:13
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 5. direct-- or, "make plain"; wicked ways are not plain (Pr 13:17).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:14
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 6. deliver them-- that is, from evil, which the wicked suffer by their own doings (Pr 5:22; Psa 9:16).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:15
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 7. expectation ... perish-- for death cuts short all his plans (Lu 16:25). hope of unjust-- better, "hope of wealth," or "power" (compare Is 40:29, Hebrew). This gives an advance on the sentiment of the first clause. Even hopes of gain die with him.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:16
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 8. Perhaps the trouble prepared by the wicked, and which he inherits (compare Pr 11:6).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:17
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 9. (Compare Psa 35:16; Da 11:32). The just is saved by superior discernment.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:18–19
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 10-11. The last may be a reason for the first. Together, they set forth the relative moral worth of good and bad men.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:20
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 12. despiseth-- or, "reviles," a course contrasted with the prudent silence of the wise. holdeth his peace-- as if neither hearing nor telling.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:21
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 13. tale-bearer-- (Compare Margin), one trading as a peddler in scandal, whose propensity to talk leads him to betray confidence.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:22
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 14. counsel-- the art of governing (Pr 1:5). counsellors-- literally, "one giving counsel"; the participle used as a collective.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:23
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 15. (Compare Pr 6:1). suretiship-- (Compare Margin), the actors put for the action, which may be lawfully hated.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:24
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 16. retaineth-- or literally, "lay hold of as a support." Honor is to a feeble woman thus as valuable as riches to men.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:25
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 17. merciful-- kind to others; opposed to cruel. Such benefit themselves by doing good to others (compare Pr 24:5), while the cruel injure themselves as well as others. flesh-- that is, his body, by penuriousness (Col 2:23).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:26
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 18. a deceitful work-- or, "wages," which fail to satisfy, or flee away (Pr 10:2; 23:5). sure reward-- or, "gain," as from trading (Ho 10:12; Ga 6:8, 9).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:27
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 19. Inference from Pr 11:18 (compare Pr 11:5, 6; 10:16).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:28
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 20. (Compare Pr 11:5). froward-- as in Pr 2:15, opposed to the simplicity and purity of the upright. in their way-- or, "conduct."

entries.title: Proverbs 31:29
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 21. The combined power of the wicked cannot free them from just punishment, while the unaided children of the righteous find deliverance by reason of their pious relationship (Psa 37:25, 26).

entries.title: Proverbs 31:30
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 22. Jewels were often suspended from the nose (Ge 24:47; Is 3:21). Thus adorned, a hog disgusts less than a fair and indiscreet woman.

entries.title: Proverbs 31:31
entries.author: Robert Jamieson, A. R. Fausset, and David Brown
entries.content: 23. (Compare Pr 10:28). wrath-- is that of God.
